agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (Context A) with Sales/Order Management (Context B) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This is a cross-bounded-context coupling request that fundamentally changes the autonomy of Context B, which currently accepts orders without checking Context A.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. This is NOT a single bounded context — it spans two, which is the core tension.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer (>= 0) representing the current quantity of a Product available in the warehouse. Owned by Context A (Warehouse Management)."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase one or more products. Owned by Context B (Sales/Order Management)."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A single line item within an Order, referencing a Product ID and a desired Quantity."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"A transient hold on StockLevel units committed to a pending order, preventing overselling. Does NOT currently exist in either context — would need to be introduced."},{"term":"Stock Available Indicator","definition":"A read-model projection of StockLevel from Context A, consumed by Context B for display purposes. Must be eventually consistent, NOT treated as authoritative for blocking decisions."},{"term":"Order Blocking","definition":"The proposed behavior of rejecting or holding an Order when StockLevel is insufficient. Introduces a synchronous coupling from Context B to Context A."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel for a Product cannot go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Physical constraint — you cannot ship items that do not exist in the warehouse. If violated, the system state is corrupt.","testable_assertion":"After any operation, StockLevel >= 0 for every Product."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An Order placed against insufficient stock must not proceed to fulfillment without resolution (back-order, partial ship, or cancellation).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Shipping what you don't have is a fulfillment integrity violation — it creates undeliverable commitments.","testable_assertion":"No Order transitions to 'Fulfilling' state if any OrderLine's Quantity exceeds available (unreserved) StockLevel."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Stock sufficiency checks must account for already-reserved stock, not just raw StockLevel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Without reservations, two concurrent orders could both see StockLevel=1 and both be accepted, violating BR-1 during pick. This is a data consistency invariant.","testable_assertion":"Available stock for blocking decisions = StockLevel - SUM(pending reservations). This value is never negative."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The 'stock available' indicator shown to the sales team is eventually consistent and must NOT be used as the authoritative source for order blocking decisions.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a UX/policy decision. The indicator is a read-model projection. A VP could decide to show approximate stock, hide it, or add a staleness warning. The blocking decision must use a strongly consistent check.","testable_assertion":"The stock indicator may show stale data (within an acceptable window), but order blocking always queries the authoritative stock+reservation state."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Context B (Sales) must not directly mutate StockLevel in Context A (Warehouse). Integration must occur via domain events or an anti-corruption layer.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Bounded context autonomy is a structural integrity constraint. If Context B writes directly to Context A's aggregates, you lose independent deployability, you break aggregate consistency boundaries, and you create hidden coupling that corrupts both models over time.","testable_assertion":"No write path exists from Sales code to the StockLevel aggregate. All cross-context communication goes through published events or a defined integration API."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Order blocking is a synchronous gate at order placement time — it requires a request/response interaction with Context A, not just an async event.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a policy choice. The business could alternatively accept all orders and reconcile asynchronously (back-order model). Blocking at placement time is one valid strategy.","testable_assertion":"When an Order is submitted, a synchronous call to the stock reservation service either confirms reservation or rejects the order before it reaches 'Placed' state."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer submits an Order with OrderLines totaling Quantity Q for a Product with available stock (StockLevel minus existing reservations) >= Q","when":"The order is placed","then":"The order is accepted, stock is reserved (available stock decreases by Q), and the order reaches 'Placed' state."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A customer submits an Order with OrderLines totaling Quantity Q for a Product with available stock < Q","when":"The order is placed","then":"The order is blocked/rejected with a clear reason ('Insufficient stock for Product X'), and no stock reservation is created."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Two customers concurrently submit orders that each require Quantity Q, and available stock = Q (only enough for one)","when":"Both orders are placed simultaneously","then":"Exactly one order is accepted and one is rejected. StockLevel never goes below zero. No double-reservation occurs."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A sales team member views the order entry screen","when":"StockLevel changes in Context A","then":"The 'stock available' indicator updates within an acceptable latency window (define SLA, e.g., < 5 seconds) without requiring a page refresh."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"The Warehouse Management service is temporarily unavailable","when":"A customer attempts to place an order","then":"The order is NOT silently accepted. The system either queues the order for retry or returns a clear 'temporarily unable to verify stock' message. No order proceeds to fulfillment without stock verification."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An order is accepted and stock reserved","when":"The order is later cancelled before fulfillment","then":"The reservation is released and available stock increases by the reserved quantity."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Race condition: two orders for the last unit of stock arrive simultaneously","expected_behavior":"Exactly one succeeds via optimistic locking or serialized reservation. The other receives a clear rejection. StockLevel never goes negative."},{"scenario":"Warehouse service is down during order placement","expected_behavior":"Order placement fails gracefully with a retryable error. The system must NOT default to accepting orders without stock verification, as this would violate BR-2."},{"scenario":"Stock is received (supplier delivery) between a customer seeing 'out of stock' and placing the order","expected_behavior":"The order should succeed because the blocking decision is made at placement time against current state, not against the stale indicator the customer saw."},{"scenario":"Partial stock availability — Order has 3 lines, 2 have stock, 1 does not","expected_behavior":"Business must decide: reject entire order (atomic) or accept partial? This is unspecified. Recommend atomic rejection with clear messaging per line item."},{"scenario":"Stock reservation expires (order placed but never fulfilled or cancelled)","expected_behavior":"Reservations must have a TTL or be tied to order lifecycle. Zombie reservations permanently reduce available stock, creating phantom unavailability."},{"scenario":"Product exists in Context B but not yet in Context A (new product, no warehouse record)","expected_behavior":"Must be treated as zero stock, not as an error. The order should be blocked with 'Product not yet available' rather than a system error."},{"scenario":"Bulk order that exceeds total warehouse capacity","expected_behavior":"Blocked like any other insufficient-stock scenario. No special handling unless business defines a 'special order' workflow."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Prevents overselling, which causes costly order cancellations, refunds, and customer compensation. However, false rejections (due to stale data or overly aggressive blocking) directly lose revenue. The reservation mechanism must be tuned to minimize both.","operational":"Introduces a synchronous runtime dependency between Sales and Warehouse systems. Warehouse service downtime now blocks order placement — this is a significant operational coupling that did not exist before. Requires SLA alignment between both systems and potentially a circuit breaker / degradation strategy.","user":"Sales team gains real-time stock visibility (positive). Customers may experience order rejections they didn't before (negative UX change — previously all orders were accepted). Requires clear error messaging and potentially a 'notify me when back in stock' flow.","risk":"HIGH — This integration introduces tight coupling between two previously independent bounded contexts. The primary risks are: (1) cascading failures if Warehouse is unavailable, (2) race conditions leading to overselling or false rejections, (3) domain model pollution if stock concepts leak into the Sales context, (4) reservation leaks if lifecycle management is incomplete.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Will now experience order rejections at placement time. Previously, all orders were accepted (even if unfulfillable). Better long-term experience but worse immediate UX if messaging is poor."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains real-time stock indicator (positive). May need to handle customer complaints about rejected orders. Needs training on new workflow."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Their system now has an SLA obligation to Sales. Downtime or slow responses directly impact order flow. Must maintain reservation lifecycle."},{"group":"Engineering / Platform","impact":"Must build and maintain a cross-context integration layer (anti-corruption layer, event bus, or API gateway). Must handle distributed system failure modes."},{"group":"Finance","impact":"Reduced refunds and cancellation costs. But if blocking is too aggressive, lost revenue from false rejections."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec has a fundamental contradiction and multiple ambiguities. The request says 'automatically block if stock is insufficient' (synchronous coupling) while Context B is defined as accepting orders 'without checking Context A' (autonomous). This is a contradiction in the current design — the feature fundamentally changes Context B's contract. Additionally, the spec does not define: partial fulfillment policy, reservation TTL, degradation behavior when Warehouse is down, or whether 'block' means reject vs. hold for manual review. These are critical business decisions that cannot be guessed.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 is an absolute invariant","Cross-context writes must go through an integration layer, not direct mutation","Concurrent access requires reservation mechanism to prevent overselling","The stock indicator must be eventually consistent, not synchronous"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether 'block' means hard reject or soft hold for review","Partial order fulfillment policy (atomic vs. partial accept)","Degradation behavior when Warehouse service is unavailable","Reservation TTL and lifecycle management policy","Acceptable staleness window for the stock indicator"],"assumptions":["The business intends a synchronous blocking gate, not an async reconciliation model","Orders should be atomically accepted or rejected (not partially)","Stock reservations are a new concept that must be introduced","The stock indicator is a read-model projection, not a live query"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Context B is currently defined as accepting orders 'without checking Context A.' The feature request fundamentally changes this contract by requiring synchronous stock verification at order placement. This is not an incremental change — it redefines Context B's bounded context boundary and autonomy model. The team must explicitly acknowledge this architectural shift.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Sales Team","Customers"],"options":["A: Synchronous blocking at order placement (as requested) — Context B loses autonomy, gains correctness","B: Async reconciliation — Context B stays autonomous, accepts all orders, then reconciles against stock post-placement (back-order model)","C: Hybrid — Accept order optimistically, reserve stock async within a short window, cancel if reservation fails"],"recommendation":"Option C (Hybrid) preserves Context B's autonomy while achieving the business goal. But this requires explicit business stakeholder sign-off because the customer experience differs significantly between options. ESCALATE to product owner."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Automatically block' is undefined. Does 'block' mean (a) hard reject with error, (b) hold in a 'Pending Stock Verification' state for manual review, or (c) accept with a warning flag? Each has radically different domain implications and customer experience.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Customers","Engineering"],"options":["A: Hard reject — order is refused, customer must retry","B: Soft hold — order enters a review queue","C: Accept with warning — order proceeds but flagged"],"recommendation":"Clarify with product owner. Hard reject is simplest but worst UX. Recommend soft hold with customer notification."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No policy defined for partial stock availability. If an order has 5 lines and only 3 have sufficient stock, should the entire order be rejected, or should it be partially fulfilled? This is a critical business decision that affects customer experience and warehouse operations.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Warehouse Team","Sales Team"],"options":["A: Atomic — reject entire order if any line lacks stock","B: Partial — accept available lines, block/back-order unavailable lines"],"recommendation":"Start with atomic rejection (simpler, safer). Add partial fulfillment as a follow-up feature if business demands it."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No degradation policy for Warehouse service unavailability. If Context A is down, should Context B (a) reject all orders, (b) accept all orders optimistically, or (c) queue orders for deferred verification? This is a critical operational decision.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering","Warehouse Team"],"options":["A: Fail closed — reject orders (safe but revenue-blocking)","B: Fail open — accept orders (risky, may oversell)","C: Queue with timeout — hold orders for N minutes, then fail"],"recommendation":"Fail closed with clear customer messaging and alerting to ops team. Overselling is worse than temporary order rejection."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Direct coupling of Context B to Context A's internal state without an anti-corruption layer would violate bounded context boundaries","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"},{"violation":"Any implementation that checks raw StockLevel without accounting for existing reservations will allow overselling under concurrent load","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"Using the eventually-consistent stock indicator as the authoritative source for blocking decisions will lead to race conditions and incorrect blocks/accepts","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"}]}

This is a cross-bounded-context coupling request that fundamentally changes the autonomy of Context B, which currently accepts orders without checking Context A.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management.
